title: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo
section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.title: Genetic and Connective Tissue Associations in Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP)
items.content: Mitral valve prolapse is often associated with genetic factors and connective tissue disorders. A meta-analysis identified 14 genetic loci linked to MVP, including genes like LMCD1, SPTBN1, and TGFB2. MVP frequently occurs in heritable connective tissue disorders such as Marfan syndrome, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.
keyPoints: Genetic associations with 14 loci identified in a meta-analysis of six genome-wide association studies involving 4884 MVP cases.
Candidate genes include LMCD1, SPTBN1, TGFB2, NMB, ALPK3, LMNA, FLNC, and FLNA.
MVP is common in connective tissue disorders like Marfan syndrome, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.title: Pathophysiology and Complications of Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP)
items.content: In MVP, myxomatous degeneration often affects the mitral valve, leading to regurgitation. This can involve elongated or ruptured chordae tendineae and a dilated annulus. The condition may cause stress on papillary muscles, potentially resulting in ischemia, infarction, and fibrosis, which are visible as late gadolinium enhancement on cardiac MRI.
keyPoints: Myxomatous degeneration is common in MVP, affecting the mitral valve and sometimes tricuspid and aortic valves.
Prolapse can involve one or both leaflets, often more so the posterior than anterior.
Stress on papillary muscles may lead to ischemia, infarction, and fibrosis, visible as late gadolinium enhancement on MRI.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.title: Mitral Valve Prolapse (MVP) Clinical Course and Progression
items.content: MVP is most common in women aged 15-30, with a benign course. It can progress over years or worsen rapidly due to chordal rupture or endocarditis.
keyPoints: More common in women than men
Occurs most frequently between ages 15 and 30
Clinical course often benign but may require surgical treatment in older patients (>50) with severe MR
Progression can be slow over years or rapid due to chordal rupture or endocarditis
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Arrhythmias and Sudden Death Risk in MVP Patients
items.content: MVP can cause arrhythmias like ventricular premature contractions, paroxysmal tachycardia, and AF. Sudden death is rare but more likely with severe MR and depressed LV function.
keyPoints: Common arrhythmias include ventricular premature contractions, paroxysmal supraventricular and ventricular tachycardia, atrial fibrillation
Sudden death is very rare, occurs most often in patients with severe MR and depressed LV systolic function
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Auscultation Findings in MVP
items.content: MVP is characterized by a mid- or late systolic click followed by a high-pitched murmur. The timing of these sounds changes with maneuvers affecting LV volume.
keyPoints: Systolic click occurs 0.14 s or more after S1, generated by tensing chordae tendineae or prolapsing leaflet
Click may be followed by a mid-late systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur
Murmur radiation depends on involved leaflet: posterior (to base), anterior (to axilla and back)
Click-murmur complex changes with maneuvers affecting LV volume
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.title: Echocardiographic Criteria for MVP Diagnosis
items.content: Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) is essential in diagnosing mitral valve prolapse (MVP). A key diagnostic criterion involves the systolic displacement of the mitral valve leaflets.
keyPoints: Systolic displacement of the mitral valve leaflets by at least 2 mm into the left atrium superior to the plane of the mitral annulus is a useful echocardiographic definition of MVP.
Prolapse can occur in one or both leaflets.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.title: Indications for Transesophageal Echocardiography (TEE) in MVP
items.content: Transesophageal echocardiography is indicated when detailed anatomic information of the mitral valve is required, particularly useful during surgical or transcatheter interventions.
keyPoints: TEE provides more accurate anatomical details than TTE.
It is routinely used for intraoperative guidance during valve repair.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.title: Transcatheter Edge-to-Edge Repair (TEER) in MVP
items.content: TEER is a viable option for treating symptomatic patients with severe primary mitral regurgitation due to MVP who are at high surgical risk.
keyPoints: TEER involves grasping the anterior and posterior leaflets together using commercially available systems.
Most often, MR severity is reduced but not eliminated; however, symptom status and LV function indices can improve.
Hospital mortality rates following TEER are reported to be <2%.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.question: Which of the following genetic loci have been associated with mitral valve prolapse (MVP) according to a meta-analysis?
options: LMCD1, SPTBN1, LTBP2, TGFB2, NMB, and ALPK3
BRCA1, BRCA2, TP53, EGFR, HER2, and MYC
APOE, PSEN1, APP, MAPT, GRN, and TREM2
CFTR, DMD, FBN1, HBB, HEXA, and PAH
items.explanation: The meta-analysis identified these genetic loci as associated with MVP.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.question: What is the most common abnormality leading to primary mitral regurgitation (MR) and mitral valve repair surgery?
items.explanation: The passage states that MVP is the most common abnormality leading to primary MR and mitral valve repair surgery.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT commonly associated with mitral valve prolapse (MVP)?
items.explanation: The passage associates MVP with Marfan syndrome, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, but not hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.question: Which age group is most frequently affected by mitral valve prolapse (MVP)?
items.explanation: MVP occurs most frequently between the ages of 15 and 30 years.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: What is a common auscultatory finding in patients with mitral valve prolapse?
options: Mid-systolic click followed by a systolic murmur
items.explanation: A frequent finding is the mid- or late (nonejection) systolic click, often followed by a high-pitched, mid-late systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: In which direction does the jet of mitral regurgitation (MR) typically flow with posterior leaflet prolapse in MVP?
options: Anteriorly towards the base of the heart
items.explanation: With posterior leaflet prolapse, the jet of MR is directed anteriorly and the murmur will radiate to the base of the heart.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which intervention can diminish the mid-systolic click-murmur complex in MVP?
items.explanation: Squatting increases LV volume, which diminishes the propensity of the leaflet to prolapse and delays or eliminates the click-murmur complex.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.question: Which echocardiographic finding is used to define mitral valve prolapse?
options: Systolic displacement of the mitral valve leaflets by at least 2 mm into the left atrium superior to the plane of the mitral annulus.
Diastolic displacement of the mitral valve leaflets by at least 1 mm into the left ventricle inferior to the plane of the mitral annulus.
Systolic displacement of the aortic valve leaflets by at least 2 mm into the left atrium superior to the plane of the aortic annulus.
Diastolic displacement of the tricuspid valve leaflets by at least 1 mm into the right ventricle inferior to the plane of the tricuspid annulus.
items.explanation: Mitral valve prolapse is defined by systolic displacement of the mitral valve leaflets by at least 2 mm into the left atrium superior to the plane of the mitral annulus.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.question: What is a common ECG finding in patients with mitral valve prolapse?
options: Biphasic or inverted T waves in leads II, III, and aVF.
Pathological Q waves in leads I and aVL.
Sinus bradycardia with prolonged PR interval.
items.explanation: The ECG may show biphasic or inverted T waves in leads II, III, and aVF in patients with mitral valve prolapse.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.question: Which imaging modality is indicated for more accurate anatomic information of the mitral valve?
items.explanation: Transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) is indicated when more accurate anatomic information of the mitral valve is required.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.question: What is a potential benefit of transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER) in patients with severe primary MR due to MVP?
options: Improvement in symptom status and indices of left ventricular size and function.
Reduction in the need for anticoagulation therapy.
items.explanation: TEER can improve symptom status and indices of left ventricular size and function, although it usually reduces but does not eliminate mitral regurgitation severity.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.statement: Mitral valve prolapse is frequently found in patients with heritable disorders of connective tissue.
items.explanation: The passage mentions MVP as a frequent finding in conditions like Marfan syndrome, osteogenesis imperfecta, and Ehlers-Danlos syndrome.

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.statement: MVP is primarily caused by coronary artery disease.
items.explanation: The passage does not list coronary artery disease as a primary cause of MVP; it mentions other causes like myxomatous degeneration and genetic factors.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.statement: Most patients with mitral valve prolapse remain asymptomatic throughout their lives.
items.explanation: Most patients are asymptomatic and remain so for their entire lives.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Sudden death is a common complication of mitral valve prolapse.
items.explanation: Sudden death is a very rare complication in MVP, occurring most often in patients with severe MR and depressed LV systolic function.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.statement: Transcatheter replacement devices for mitral valve are approved for use in the United States.
items.explanation: Transcatheter replacement devices remain under active investigation and are not yet approved for use in the United States.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.statement: Mitral valve repair is preferred over replacement in patients with MVP or flail mitral leaflet.
items.explanation: Mitral valve repair is preferred over replacement for patients with MVP or a flail mitral leaflet.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.subtopic: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE
items.assertion: MVP can lead to excessive stress on the papillary muscles.
items.reason: This stress may result in localized ischemia, infarction, and replacement fibrosis.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion as described in the passage.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T. O'Gara, Joseph Loscalzo | MITRAL VALVE PROLAPSE

items.assertion: Mitral valve prolapse can lead to arrhythmias.
items.reason: Arrhythmias are reported in MVP patients, including ventricular premature contractions and atrial fibrillation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Infective endocarditis can occur in patients with mitral valve prolapse.
items.reason: Endocarditis is associated with leaflet thickening and/or mitral regurgitation.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · CLINICAL FEATURES

items.assertion: Exercise testing can be useful in assessing the dynamic nature of mitral regurgitation.
items.reason: It is often combined with rest and immediate poststress TTE to assess left ventricular and right ventricular function and pulmonary artery pressures.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why exercise testing is useful.
items.reference: Mitral Valve Prolapse Patrick T O Gara Joseph Loscalzo · LABORATORY EXAMINATION | Mitral Valve Prolapse

items.assertion: Infective endocarditis prophylaxis is not indicated for patients with mitral valve prolapse.
items.reason: Prophylaxis is only recommended for patients with a prior history of endocarditis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because infective endocarditis prophylaxis is indeed indicated for patients with a prior history of endocarditis.
